The per-effect intervals enter the *value* of each row: for the correlation and directional rungs only the point estimates are used, so the per-effect intervals do not affect those scores at all and the bootstrap is their sole source of an error bar; for the inferential-agreement and TOST rungs the per-effect intervals set each row's significance or equivalence label, which the bootstrap then pools and resamples. In all cases the bootstrap holds each intervention's estimate fixed at its point value: it propagates the *which-interventions* uncertainty into the bars but **not** the within-effect sampling noise.

**A noted alternative.** Folding both layers into a single bar would require a *two-level* bootstrap — in each replicate, resample the interventions *and* perturb each drawn effect by its standard error (drawing the effect from a normal centred on the estimate with that SE). We adopt the single-level (point-estimate) cluster bootstrap as the default because, with only sixteen interventions, the *which-interventions* term dominates the uncertainty in any ranking, while the human study is powered such that each effect's standard error is comparatively small relative to the treatment-to-treatment spread. The per-effect CIs and PIs remain reported in the per-outcome breakdowns as the finer, within-effect layer.

**Scope across panels.** The ATE leaderboard scores interventions, so its bootstrap resamples interventions and is available to **every tier** — the resampling needs only one predicted effect per intervention, which all tiers supply (including effect-only Tier 3). The subgroup and distributional leaderboards are restricted to Tier 1, but for a different reason: their *metrics* (interaction models, response-shape comparisons) require respondent-level data, not the bootstrap. The distributional metrics are computed in the control condition only and have no intervention contrast, so that panel necessarily resamples outcomes rather than interventions; with a handful of outcomes those intervals are correspondingly coarser and are read with that caveat.

Registered approach characteristics (open- vs. closed-weight, persona source, administration protocol, fine-tuned vs. not, …) are joined to the leaderboard for a descriptive, hypothesis-generating analysis of which design choices accompany better predictions—preregistered as exploratory, and restricted to publicly disclosed features (Class C entries are excluded from this analysis, not from the leaderboard).

::: {.callout-note}
**Exploratory by design.** With a modest number of teams, the relationship between approach characteristics and predictive performance is reported **descriptively** (rankings, group comparisons, visual patterns). We do not preregister inferential tests of feature effects; the cross-team feature analysis is hypothesis-generating, intended to surface which design choices appear to matter for future, larger benchmarks.
:::

#### Scoring functions

The cross-team analysis reuses the comparison functions specified under *Comparison functions*, above, and adds the helpers below. The scoring helpers (in `R/functions/statistics.R`) are shown in full and explained before each; the two plotting helpers (in `R/functions/plots.R`) are described but not reproduced here.

`pooled_metrics()` computes the ATE-recovery ladder on intervention × outcome pairs **pooled across all outcomes**: directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, the noise-corrected companion r~adj~ (via `adjusted_metrics()`, see *Comparison functions*), the Fisher-z per-outcome-averaged correlations (via `fisher_z_avg()`; the pooled-vs-averaged divergence diagnostic, see *Scoring principle*), inferential agreement, and TOST equivalence with the fixed per-outcome bounds (`tost_delta`). It mirrors the original `compare_estimates()` but operates on the pooled set of pairs rather than a single outcome; pooled RMSE is deliberately omitted, as it is not comparable across outcome scales, but `include_rmse = TRUE` adds RMSE and RMSE~adj~ for the per-outcome and per-intervention breakdowns where scale is constant. It expects pre-joined pairs with the reference (Human 1) columns `estimate_h`, `se_h`, `p_adj_h` and the submission columns `estimate_l`, `se_l`, `infer_l`. `pi_metrics()` scores the Tier-2/3 uncertainty intervals (coverage + Winkler interval score); `cluster_boot_diff()` implements the paired-replicate decision rule for ranking claims.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-pooled-metrics
print_a_function_from_file("pooled_metrics")
```

`signed_metrics()` is the reduced ladder for the subgroup leaderboard, where only the signed-effect rungs are pooled (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r). This matches Section 2, above, which reports these three on estimate-only pairs (no standard error or inferential category).

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-signed-metrics
print_a_function_from_file("signed_metrics")
```

`cluster_boot()` attaches the uncertainty interval described above. It resamples the levels of a clustering variable (interventions for the ATE and subgroup panels, outcomes for the distributional panel) with replacement, recomputes the supplied metric function on each replicate, and returns the point estimate together with percentile confidence bounds in long form (one row per metric, with `value`, `lo`, `hi`). It is generic: the metric function is passed in, so the same bootstrap serves every panel.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-cluster-boot
print_a_function_from_file("cluster_boot")
```

`metrics_by_group()` scores within each level of a grouping variable instead of pooling everything into one number: with `group = "outcome"` it returns one ladder per outcome (scored across the interventions), and with `group = "condition"` one ladder per intervention (scored across the outcomes). It drives the by-outcome and by-intervention distribution figures. Per intervention the score runs across `outcomes_continuous`, all on the same 0–100 slider scale, so the correlation rungs are not scale-confounded.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-metrics-by-group
print_a_function_from_file("metrics_by_group")
```

`summarise_field()` reduces the per-approach scores to the field-level summary printed beside the headline figure: mean, median, sd, min, and max of each metric across approaches (excluding the reference row).

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-summarise-field
print_a_function_from_file("summarise_field")
```

The plotting helpers in `R/functions/plots.R` render these scores on a shared MetBrewer *Juarez* palette. `plot_pred_scatter()` draws predicted against human estimates pooled over approaches, with the identity line, the human replication reference marked, and optionally zero-centred symmetric axes; `calibration_overlay()` adds the average field and human calibration regressions and a corner readout of correlation and calibration coefficients on top of it. `plot_field_forest_single()` draws the single-metric ranking: approaches ordered by the metric with cluster-bootstrap intervals as horizontal bars, the noise-corrected companion as open points, the reference pinned on top, and an all-approaches summary row at the bottom (field density, mean with its 95% CI, and the mean of the noise-corrected companion as an open point). `plot_metric_distribution()` draws the distribution of approaches as a half-violin of the field over a dot strip — pooled (with mean, median, and optionally the mean's CI) or one row per group (outcome, intervention, or moderator, with the field mean and 95% CI per row). `plot_density_overlay()` overlays every Tier-1 approach's response density with the human reference and replication halves, one facet per outcome, with an optional per-facet metric stamp.

The remainder of this section demonstrates the code on placeholder data: **30 mock "teams"**, each a stratified bootstrap resample (within condition) of the single individual-level placeholder dataset, stacked with a `team` label — enough approaches to give the field distributions a meaningful shape. To exercise all three tier code paths, 28 teams enter at Tier 1 (individual-level), one is collapsed into a Tier 2 cell-level file, and one into a Tier 3 effect-level file. All of this placeholder data—including the resampled teams and the collapsed Tier 2/3 files—is built in the simulation script (`R/simulate_data_preregistration.R`), not here; this document only reads it. Because every mock team is just a resample of the same dataset, all approaches are random noise relative to the human reference: on placeholder data the field distributions therefore cluster near the null, and the figures only show that the code runs — real submissions will spread out. The adjusted correlation r~adj~ behaves accordingly: with no true signal, the reference effects' observed spread is all noise, so its guard (estimated true variance ≤ 0 → NA) binds for many mock teams and the surviving values are unstable — the intended behaviour, demonstrated rather than hidden. The code applies to real submissions without modification.

```{r}
#| label: variables

outcomes_primary   <- "trust_multidimensional"
outcomes_secondary <- c("trust_post", "distrust_post",
                         "funding_perceptions", "policy_role_mean", "inst_trust_mean")
outcomes_tertiary  <- c("belief_post", "concern_mean", "policy_general",
                        "policy_specific_mean", "behavior_mean")
outcomes_continuous <- c(outcomes_primary, outcomes_secondary, outcomes_tertiary)

# The two behavioral outcomes enter the pooled cross-team ladder alongside the
# 0-100 sliders (as in v1's pooled ATE row): donation in dollars via the OLS
# model, newsletter signup via logistic marginal effects on the probability
# scale. Scale-bound quantities (RMSE, the Winkler interval score, the
# by-intervention ladder) stay on `outcomes_continuous` only.
outcomes_behavioral <- c("donation_ams", "newsletter_signup")
outcomes_pooled     <- c(outcomes_continuous, outcomes_behavioral)

# Fixed, prespecified TOST equivalence bounds in outcome units — 3% of each
# scale's range: 3 points on the 0-100 sliders, $0.30 on the 0-10 donation,
# 3 percentage points on the signup proportion. Fixed rather than
# |ATE_h|-relative (v1's bound): a bound computed from the noisy estimate
# under test is not a valid alpha-level procedure, and the design analysis
# below shows the relative bound has no dynamic range at this design's
# reference SEs.
tost_deltas <- c(
  set_names(rep(3, length(outcomes_continuous)), outcomes_continuous),
  donation_ams = 0.3, newsletter_signup = 0.03
)

# Scale ranges, for expressing effects in percentage points of each outcome's
# range in the figures — following @hewittPredictingResultsSocial2025, whose
# predicted-vs-observed figure reports effects in pp on the original scales
# and deliberately avoids SD standardization (an under-dispersed submission
# would corrupt an SD denominator). The 0-100 sliders are already in pp; only
# the donation (0-10 dollars) and signup probability (0-1) rescale.
scale_range <- c(
  set_names(rep(100, length(outcomes_continuous)), outcomes_continuous),
  donation_ams = 10, newsletter_signup = 1
)

# Bootstrap replicates: the PREREGISTERED value for the real-data run is
# 2,000. This placeholder render is a demonstration of the procedure, not of
# its precision, so it uses a small demo value to keep the render cheap —
# only this parameter changes when the submissions arrive, nothing else.
n_boot_real <- 2000
n_boot_demo <- 200

# Illustrative outcome subset and categorical moderators — same definitions as
# Section 2 above; used for the subgroup-effects leaderboard.
outcomes_illustrative <- c("trust_multidimensional", "donation_ams",
                           "funding_perceptions", "policy_general")
moderators_cat <- c("gender", "age_band", "race", "education", "income", "party")

outcome_label_map <- c(
  trust_multidimensional = "Multidimensional trust (primary)",
  trust_post             = "Overall trust",
  distrust_post          = "Distrust",
  funding_perceptions    = "Funding perceptions",
  policy_role_mean       = "Policy role",
  inst_trust_mean        = "Institutional trust",
  belief_post            = "Climate belief",
  concern_mean           = "Climate concern",
  policy_general         = "General climate policy",
  policy_specific_mean   = "Specific climate policy",
  behavior_mean          = "Pro-climate behavior",
  donation_ams           = "Donation to AMS ($)",
  newsletter_signup      = "Newsletter signup"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: leaderboard-helpers

# Extract ATE pairs (estimate, SE, adjusted p) — same helper as the original
# preregistration's rq1-all chunk.
prep_ate <- function(df) {
  df |>
    select(condition, estimate) |>
    mutate(
      se    = if ("std.error"        %in% names(df)) df$std.error        else NA_real_,
      p_adj = if ("p.value_adjusted" %in% names(df)) df$p.value_adjusted else NA_real_
    )
}

# Inferential category from estimate sign + adjusted p-value (Tiers 1-2)
classify_sig <- function(estimate, p_adj) {
  case_when(
    !is.na(p_adj) & p_adj < .05 & estimate > 0 ~ "pos",
    !is.na(p_adj) & p_adj < .05 & estimate < 0 ~ "neg",
    TRUE                                       ~ "ns"
  )
}

control_lab <- levels(human_data$condition)[1]
z95 <- qnorm(0.975)

# Reference (Human 1) ATE models on the continuous outcomes, fit once and
# reused (the calibration regression reads the primary outcome's model).
human_models <- map(outcomes_continuous,
                    ~ run_main_treatment_model(human_data_1, outcome = .x)) |>
  set_names(outcomes_continuous)

# One ATE side (estimate, SE, BH-adjusted p) for an individual-level dataset,
# across all pooled outcomes: OLS with HC2 SEs for the continuous outcomes and
# the donation; logistic marginal effects on the probability scale for the
# binary newsletter signup (as in v1's pooled row). The binary SE is recovered
# from the marginal-effect CI.
ate_side <- function(dat, models = NULL) {
  cont <- map_dfr(c(outcomes_continuous, "donation_ams"), function(out) {
    res <- if (!is.null(models) && out %in% names(models)) models[[out]]
           else run_main_treatment_model(dat, outcome = out)
    prep_ate(res) |> mutate(outcome = out)
  })
  bin <- run_main_treatment_model_binary(dat,
                                         outcome = "newsletter_signup")$marginal_effects |>
    transmute(condition,
              estimate,
              se      = (conf.high - conf.low) / (2 * z95),
              p_adj   = p.value_adjusted,
              outcome = "newsletter_signup")
  bind_rows(cont, bin)
}

human_side <- ate_side(human_data_1, models = human_models) |>
  rename(estimate_h = estimate, se_h = se, p_adj_h = p_adj)

# Human per-condition cell sizes (named vector), for standardizing Tier-1
# submission-side inference to the human design's precision.
n_cells_h <- human_data_1 |> count(condition) |> deframe()

# Tier 1: pool ATE pairs from an individual-level dataset by refitting the
# preregistered treatment models on the submission. Submission-side INFERENCE
# (the inferential-agreement category and the TOST SE) is evaluated at the
# HUMAN design's per-arm precision: a team may simulate any pool size, so
# model-based significance would otherwise measure that choice rather than the
# method — and the Tier-1 precision requirement would mechanically hurt
# precise teams wherever a human effect is n.s. Each submission SE is rescaled
# by the two-cell design factor sqrt((1/n_h,k + 1/n_h,ctrl) /
# (1/n_l,k + 1/n_l,ctrl)) and p-values recomputed (normal approximation, BH
# within outcome, mirroring the Tier-2 path). Point estimates are untouched,
# so the correlation, directional, and RMSE rungs are unaffected. This also
# makes the inferential and TOST columns comparable across tiers: every
# submission's "significance" is evaluated at the same design precision.
build_pairs_t1 <- function(pred_data) {
  n_cells_l <- pred_data |> count(condition) |> deframe()
  fac <- sqrt((1 / n_cells_h + 1 / n_cells_h[[control_lab]]) /
              (1 / n_cells_l[names(n_cells_h)] + 1 / n_cells_l[[control_lab]]))
  ate_side(pred_data) |>
    mutate(se_std = se * unname(fac[condition])) |>
    group_by(outcome) |>
    mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(2 * pnorm(-abs(estimate / se_std)), method = "BH")) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(infer_l = classify_sig(estimate, p_adj)) |>
    select(condition, outcome, estimate_l = estimate, se_l = se_std, infer_l) |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome"))
}

# Tier 2: ATEs and SEs from submitted cell means and their 95% prediction
# intervals (mean, pi_lower, pi_upper per condition x outcome). Each cell
# interval implies an SE on the cell mean; the treatment and control cells
# combine for the between-subjects contrast. Normal-approximation p-values,
# BH-adjusted within outcome to mirror the Tier 1 models.
build_pairs_t2 <- function(cells_main) {
  cells_main |>
    mutate(
      condition = as.character(condition),
      se_cell   = (pi_upper - pi_lower) / (2 * z95)
    ) |>
    group_by(outcome) |>
    group_modify(function(cells, key) {
      ctrl <- cells |> filter(condition == control_lab)
      cells |>
        filter(condition != control_lab) |>
        mutate(
          estimate_l = mean - ctrl$mean,
          se_l       = sqrt(se_cell^2 + ctrl$se_cell^2),
          p_adj      = p.adjust(2 * pnorm(-abs(estimate_l / se_l)), method = "BH"),
          infer_l    = classify_sig(estimate_l, p_adj)
        ) |>
        select(condition, estimate_l, se_l, infer_l)
    }) |>
    ungroup() |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome"))
}

# Tier 3: submitted ATEs taken as-is; inference from the submitted 95%
# uncertainty interval (significant iff the interval excludes zero, the direct
# analog of a two-sided test at alpha = .05); implied SE from the interval
# width for the TOST computation.
build_pairs_t3 <- function(effects) {
  effects |>
    mutate(
      condition  = as.character(condition),
      estimate_l = ate,
      se_l       = (pi_upper - pi_lower) / (2 * z95),
      infer_l    = case_when(
        pi_lower > 0 ~ "pos",
        pi_upper < 0 ~ "neg",
        TRUE         ~ "ns"
      )
    ) |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
    select(condition, outcome, estimate_l, se_l, infer_l,
           estimate_h, se_h, p_adj_h)
}

# `pooled_metrics()`, `signed_metrics()`, and `cluster_boot()` are defined in
# R/functions/statistics.R and shown under "Scoring functions" above.

# One leaderboard row: pooled ladder + calibration slope and R^2 on the
# primary outcome (HC2-robust inference: the outcome side of the calibration
# regression carries known, condition-varying sampling error) + the two
# interval scores for Tiers 2-3 (pi_metrics(); the Winkler score is
# scale-bound, so it runs on the 0-100 outcomes only). Interval scores are NA
# for Tier 1 (its SEs are sampling, not epistemic, uncertainty) and for the
# reference rows.
leaderboard_row <- function(pairs, submission, tier, disclosure) {
  calib <- run_calibration(
    human_models[[outcomes_primary]],
    pairs |> filter(outcome == outcomes_primary) |>
      select(condition, estimate = estimate_l),
    robust = TRUE
  )
  pi <- if (tier %in% c("2", "3"))
    pi_metrics(pairs |> filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous))
  else tibble(pi_coverage_pct = NA_real_, interval_score = NA_real_)
  pooled_metrics(pairs, tost_delta = tost_deltas) |>
    mutate(submission = submission, tier = tier, disclosure = disclosure,
           beta = calib$beta, calib_r2 = calib$r_squared,
           pi_coverage_pct = pi$pi_coverage_pct,
           interval_score  = pi$interval_score)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: mock-submissions

# Mock Tier 2 (cell-level) and Tier 3 (effect-level) submissions, generated
# outside this script alongside the other placeholder data (see
# R/simulate_data_preregistration.R) so that no mock-data simulation lives in
# the preregistration itself. Tier 2 collapses one placeholder team to the
# cell-level schema (*_cells_main.csv); Tier 3 gives ATEs vs. control with a
# widened 95% uncertainty interval standing in for a team's epistemic uncertainty.
cells_t2   <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/mock_submission_tier2_cells.rds"))
effects_t3 <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/mock_submission_tier3_effects.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| label: leaderboard-build

# 30 mock submissions plus the fixed human replication reference (Human 1 vs. Human 2).
# Teams 1-28 enter as individual-level Tier 1 data; team 29 as a Tier 2
# cell-level file and team 30 as a Tier 3 effect-level file, so all three tier
# code paths are exercised. Each submission's ATE pairs are built once here and
# reused by the headline and breakdown figures, the leaderboard table, and the
# companion tables.
tier1_ids <- 1:28

# Cached: the 29 Tier-1 builds each refit the treatment models incl. the
# logistic marginal effects for the newsletter outcome, the slow step of a
# cold render.
pairs_list <- cached("amendment_pairs_list", {
  c(
    set_names(
      map(tier1_ids,
          ~ build_pairs_t1(llm_data_teams |> filter(team == paste0("team_", .x)))),
      paste0("Team ", tier1_ids)
    ),
    list(
      "Team 29"           = build_pairs_t2(cells_t2),
      "Team 30"           = build_pairs_t3(effects_t3),
      "Human replication" = build_pairs_t1(human_data_2)
    )
  )
})

set.seed(2026)
submission_meta <- tibble(
  submission = c(paste0("Team ", 1:30), "Human replication"),
  tier       = c(rep("1", 28), "2", "3", "—"),
  disclosure = c(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 30, replace = TRUE), "—")
)

# Floor baseline: a naive reference row for the rungs without a natural null
# (inferential %, TOST %, RMSE). "No effect" predicts a zero ATE everywhere —
# a fully naive, blinded-achievable strategy whose inferential agreement is
# whatever the share of truly null human effects hands it for free. It is a
# reference row like the reference, not a member of the field: excluded from the
# field violins and the field summary. (An oracle-mean floor — predict each
# outcome's mean human ATE everywhere — was considered and rejected: the mean
# is the in-sample least-squares constant fit to the very ATEs being scored,
# so it competes rather than floors; its interpretive content survives as the
# RMSE scale reference, the SD of the human ATEs.)
floor_pairs <- list(
  "Floor: no effect" = human_side |>
    mutate(estimate_l = 0, se_l = 0, infer_l = "ns")
)
floor_labels <- names(floor_pairs)

# Calibration is undefined for the floor (a constant prediction within the
# primary outcome -> zero-variance regressor), so their rows carry NA there.
# Its directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign, an artifact
# of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill (footnoted in the table).
floor_rows <- imap_dfr(floor_pairs, function(pr, sub) {
  pooled_metrics(pr, tost_delta = tost_deltas) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, tier = "—", disclosure = "—",
           beta = NA_real_, calib_r2 = NA_real_,
           pi_coverage_pct = NA_real_, interval_score = NA_real_)
})

leaderboard <- bind_rows(
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    m <- submission_meta |> filter(submission == sub)
    leaderboard_row(pr, sub, tier = m$tier, disclosure = m$disclosure)
  }),
  floor_rows
) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(pearson_r))
```

```{r}
#| label: ate-ladder-labels

# Display labels / panel order for the metric ladder, and the null reference
# line per metric (50 = chance for directional agreement, 0 for the
# correlations; omitted for the percentage-agreement metrics where no clean
# null exists).
ladder_labels <- c(
  directional_pct = "Directional %",
  spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
  pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
  pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
  inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
  tost_pct        = "TOST %"
)
ladder_nulls <- c("Directional %" = 50, "Spearman ρ" = 0, "Pearson r" = 0,
                  "Pearson r (adj.)" = 0)

relabel_metrics <- function(df, labels) {
  df |> mutate(metric = factor(labels[metric], levels = labels))
}
```

#### Finding 1 — How well can the field predict the experiment?

The headline question is not *who wins* but how well the current generation of approaches can predict a novel experiment at all. To keep that readable, each question gets **one key metric in the figures** — Pearson *r* for ATE recovery, Pearson *r* on the interaction estimates for subgroup recovery, and the variance ratio for response shape. The correlation is the literature's common currency [@hewittPredictingResultsSocial2025], and one shared axis across the ATE and subgroup sections makes them directly comparable; the variance ratio carries the field's one directional expectation (under-dispersion). The full preregistered ladders are computed for everything and reported in the companion tables.

@fig-headline shows the headline: the raw prediction task with the field's calibration lines and coefficients (A), and the field and its ranking on the pooled Pearson *r* (B). One rendering note on panel B: this placeholder demonstration runs the bootstrap with a small replicate count (`n_boot_demo = 200`) to keep the document cheap to build; the **preregistered value for the real-data run is `n_boot_real = 2,000`** — when the submissions arrive, only that parameter changes, nothing else about the procedure.

```{r}
#| label: fig-headline
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-cap: "Headline cross-team figure (placeholder data). (A) Predicted against human ATEs across all outcomes and approaches, in percentage points of each outcome's scale range (as in the predicted-vs-observed figure of Hewitt et al.; the 0–100 sliders are already in pp, the behavioral outcomes rescale) — one point per approach × intervention × outcome, axes centred on zero, the dashed line the identity, red crosses the human replication reference (Human 2 predicting Human 1). The solid lines are the average calibration regression of human on predicted, pooled across all outcomes: dark blue the field (per-approach fits averaged), red the human replication reference. The corner prints the mean field Pearson r (and its noise-corrected value) and the human replication's — the same pooled metric ranked in B — together with the field and human calibration coefficients (slope β, perfect = 1; intercept α, perfect = 0) with 95% CIs. (B) Approaches ranked by Pearson r pooled across all outcomes against Human 1: filled points with 95% cluster-bootstrap intervals over interventions, open points the noise-corrected r (adj.) where its guard allows a value, the human replication reference pinned on top (its value dropped as a dotted line). The bottom row summarises the field meta-analytically: the density of the approaches' scores, the diamond their mean with its 95% CI, and the open point the mean noise-corrected r. On placeholder data every approach is a noise resample of the same dataset, so the field clusters near r = 0 in panel B — no relationship to the truth, as expected of a random resample."

# (A) the raw prediction task: all approaches, all outcomes, in percentage
# points of each outcome's scale range
headline_pairs <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y)) |>
  mutate(estimate_l = estimate_l * 100 / scale_range[outcome],
         estimate_h = estimate_h * 100 / scale_range[outcome])
p_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  headline_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted ATE (pp of scale range)",
  y_lab       = "Human ATE (pp of scale range)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.15, point_size = 0.5,
  ref_alpha   = 0.6,  ref_size   = 1,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Average field / human calibration lines + a corner readout of correlation
# (r, adj.) and coefficients (β, α with CI). Mean field Pearson r and its
# noise-corrected value come from the pooled leaderboard — the same metric
# ranked in panel B — so the raw task carries its one-number summary; the
# coefficients are fit from the plotted pp pairs by calibration_overlay().
field_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(!submission %in% floor_labels,
                                  submission != "Human replication")
human_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_scatter <- p_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(headline_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = human_ld$pearson_adj)

# (B) ranked field on the key metric, with cluster-bootstrap intervals.
# n_boot_demo here; the real-data run uses n_boot_real = 2,000 (see variables).
pooled_f <- \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_deltas)
ate_forest <- cached("amendment_ate_forest", {
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    cluster_boot(pr, pooled_f, cluster = "condition", n_boot = n_boot_demo) |>
      mutate(submission = sub)
  }) |>
    relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)
})
p_forest <- plot_field_forest_single(ate_forest, tag = "B")

(p_scatter | p_forest) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.15))
```

The full pooled ladder stays scored for every approach; the field's centre and spread on each rung:

```{r}
#| label: field-pooled-compute

field_pooled <- leaderboard |>
  filter(!submission %in% floor_labels) |>   # floors are reference rows, not field members
  select(submission, all_of(names(ladder_labels))) |>
  pivot_longer(-submission, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-field-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Field summary (placeholder data): centre and spread of each pooled ATE-recovery metric across the 30 approaches (the human replication reference excluded)."

summarise_field(field_pooled) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, median, sd, min, max), decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", median = "Median",
             sd = "SD", min = "Min", max = "Max")
```

##### Selecting the best interventions

The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern; a decision-maker's question is narrower — *if I trust this approach and act on its top picks, do I get the interventions that truly work best?* `topk_selection()` scores exactly that (see *Section 1*): per outcome, each approach's top **k = 3** interventions by predicted ATE (≈ the top 20% of 16 arms, in the spirit of the intervention-selection use case that motivates @hewittPredictingResultsSocial2025) are compared with the truly best three by the human reference — the **hit rate** (share of picks that are in the true best set; chance = 3/16 ≈ 0.19) and the **regret ratio** (mean true ATE of the picked set as a share of the best achievable; 1 = perfect pick). The table shows both on the primary outcome and averaged across the continuous outcomes.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-topk
#| tbl-cap: "Top-3 intervention selection (placeholder data): per approach, the hit rate (share of its top-3 picks that are among the truly best 3; chance ≈ 0.19) and the regret ratio (mean true ATE of the picked set / best achievable; 1 = perfect), on the primary outcome and averaged across the continuous outcomes. On placeholder data picks are random, so hit rates sit near chance."

topk_table <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  scores <- topk_selection(pr, k = 3)
  bind_cols(
    scores |>
      filter(outcome == outcomes_primary) |>
      transmute(hit_primary = hit_rate, regret_primary = regret_ratio),
    scores |>
      summarise(hit_mean    = mean(hit_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
                regret_mean = mean(regret_ratio, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, .before = 1)
}) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(hit_mean))

topk_table |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(hit_primary, regret_primary, hit_mean, regret_mean),
             decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission     = "Submission",
    hit_primary    = "Hit rate (primary)",
    regret_primary = "Regret ratio (primary)",
    hit_mean       = "Hit rate (mean)",
    regret_mean    = "Regret ratio (mean)"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  )
```

##### Where the field predicts well

Split **by outcome** (one distribution per outcome, scored across the interventions) and **by intervention** (one per intervention, scored across the 0–100 continuous outcomes, so the correlations are not scale-confounded by the donation and newsletter outcomes), the key metric shows *where* the field predicts well and what resists prediction (@fig-field-by-group). The full ladders per outcome and per intervention are in @tbl-field-by-outcome and @tbl-field-by-intervention.

```{r}
#| label: field-by-group-compute

# include_rmse: scale is constant within each outcome, so RMSE and its
# noise-corrected companion RMSE_adj are defined here (not in the pooled view).
field_by_outcome <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr, "outcome",
                   f = \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_deltas,
                                           include_rmse = TRUE)) |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, tost_pct, rmse, rmse_adj),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(c(ladder_labels, rmse = "RMSE", rmse_adj = "RMSE (adj.)"))

# Scale-bound view: restricted to the 0-100 continuous outcomes (the donation
# and newsletter outcomes, on other scales, would confound the correlation
# rungs here).
field_by_intervention <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr |> filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous), "condition") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, tost_pct),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)

# Companion-table helper: field median per metric within each group level,
# with the human replication reference value in parentheses.
field_group_table <- function(field_long, group,
                              pct_metrics = c("Directional %", "Inferential %",
                                              "TOST %")) {
  fmt <- function(v, pct) if_else(is.na(v), "—",
                                  if_else(pct, sprintf("%.0f", v),
                                          sprintf("%.2f", v)))
  med <- field_long |>
    filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
    group_by(across(all_of(group)), metric) |>
    summarise(med = median(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  ref <- field_long |>
    filter(submission == "Human replication") |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, ref = value)
  med |>
    left_join(ref, by = c(group, "metric")) |>
    mutate(pct  = metric %in% pct_metrics,
           cell = paste0(fmt(med, pct), " (", fmt(ref, pct), ")")) |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, cell) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = cell)
}
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-field-by-group
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 6.5
#| fig-cap: "Where the field predicts well (placeholder data): distribution of each approach's Pearson r by outcome (A; scored across the interventions) and by intervention (B; scored across the 0–100 continuous outcomes). One dot per approach over the field's half-violin, with the field mean and its 95% CI (white diamond) on each row; red crosses mark the human replication reference, the dotted line the null."

by_outcome_r <- field_by_outcome |>
  filter(metric == "Pearson r") |>
  mutate(metric = factor("Outcomes"))
by_intervention_r <- field_by_intervention |>
  filter(metric == "Pearson r") |>
  mutate(metric = factor("Interventions"))

p_by_outcome <- plot_metric_distribution(
  by_outcome_r, x = "outcome", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Outcomes" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "A", y = "Pearson r")
p_by_intervention <- plot_metric_distribution(
  by_intervention_r, x = "condition", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Interventions" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "B", y = "Pearson r")

p_by_outcome | p_by_intervention
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-field-by-outcome
#| tbl-cap: "Field summary by outcome (placeholder data): median across approaches of each ladder metric, scored per outcome across the interventions; the human replication reference in parentheses. RMSE and RMSE (adj.) are defined here because scale is constant within an outcome."

field_group_table(field_by_outcome, "outcome") |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(outcome = "Outcome")
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-field-by-intervention
#| tbl-cap: "Field summary by intervention (placeholder data): median across approaches of each ladder metric, scored per intervention across the 0–100 continuous outcomes; the human replication reference in parentheses."

field_group_table(field_by_intervention, "condition") |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(condition = "Intervention")
```

##### Subgroup recovery

Whether an approach reproduces *who* an intervention moves, not just its average effect. For each Tier-1 submission we fit the preregistered condition × moderator interaction model (`run_moderator_model()`) across the illustrative outcomes and the six demographic moderators, then pool the interaction estimates against Human 1 (`compute_predicted = FALSE` skips the marginal-effects step, which the leaderboard does not use). Because interaction estimates require respondent-level data, this view covers Tier 1 only. As specified in Section 2 above, the signed-effect rungs are scored (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r) plus the adjusted correlation r~adj~ — emphasized here, because small subgroup cells make the reference noise non-uniform across cells (see *Adjusted correlation and RMSE*).

```{r}
#| label: subgroup-pairs

# Human-1 interaction estimates, computed once and reused for every submission.
# The cached() keys keep their historical "amendment_" prefix (the analysis is
# unchanged); they are internal identifiers for analysis/results/*.rds, not tied
# to this file's name, so renaming the document does not require a recompute.
human_mod_side <- cached("amendment_human_mod_side", {
  map_dfr(outcomes_illustrative, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(human_data_1, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        # se_h feeds the adjusted correlation; only the human reference side
        # is corrected (see "Adjusted correlation and RMSE").
        select(condition, moderator_level,
               estimate_h = estimate, se_h = std.error) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
})

build_subgroup_pairs <- function(pred_data) {
  pred_side <- map_dfr(outcomes_illustrative, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(pred_data, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        select(condition, moderator_level, estimate_l = estimate) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
  inner_join(human_mod_side, pred_side,
             by = c("condition", "moderator_level", "outcome", "moderator"))
}

# Tier-1 submissions plus the reference are eligible. Interaction pairs are built
# once per submission and reused by the subgroup figure and its companion table.
subgroup_inputs <- c(
  set_names(
    map(tier1_ids, ~ llm_data_teams |> filter(team == paste0("team_", .x))),
    paste0("Team ", tier1_ids)
  ),
  list("Human replication" = human_data_2)
)
subgroup_pairs_list <- cached("amendment_subgroup_pairs", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, build_subgroup_pairs)
})
```

@fig-subgroup-headline shows the moderation analogue of the headline figure: the raw prediction task on the interaction estimates (A), and the key metric per moderator (B) — which kinds of heterogeneity the field can predict at all. The full signed-effect ladder, pooled, is in @tbl-field-subgroup.

```{r}
#| label: fig-subgroup-headline
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 4.2
#| fig-cap: "Subgroup recovery (placeholder data, Tier 1). (A) Predicted against human condition × moderator interaction estimates pooled across all approaches and the four illustrative outcomes (Section 2 scope), in percentage points of each outcome's scale range, axes centred on zero; dashed identity line, red crosses the human replication reference. The solid lines are the average calibration regression of human on predicted (dark blue the field, red the human replication), with a corner readout of the mean field and human Pearson r (and its noise-corrected value) and calibration coefficients (β, α with 95% CI). (B) Distribution of each approach's Pearson r on the interaction estimates, per moderator (pooled across the illustrative outcomes), with the field mean and its 95% CI (white diamond) per row; red crosses the reference, dotted line the null."

moderator_label_map <- c(gender = "Gender", age_band = "Age",
                         race = "Race/ethnicity", education = "Education",
                         income = "Income", party = "Party ID")

subgroup_scatter_pairs <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                                   ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y)) |>
  mutate(estimate_l = estimate_l * 100 / scale_range[outcome],
         estimate_h = estimate_h * 100 / scale_range[outcome])
# ~37,000 pooled pairs: much lighter marks than the ATE scatter, or it reads
# as a solid blob.
p_sub_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  subgroup_scatter_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted interaction estimate (pp)",
  y_lab       = "Human interaction estimate (pp)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.05, point_size = 0.3,
  ref_alpha   = 0.2,  ref_size   = 0.5,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Mean field / human Pearson r on the interaction estimates (from the pooled
# signed-effect ladder, matching panel B), and the calibration lines + readout.
subgroup_ld <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                        ~ signed_metrics(.x) |> mutate(submission = .y))
sub_field_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission != "Human replication")
sub_human_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_sub_scatter <- p_sub_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(subgroup_scatter_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = sub_human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = sub_human_ld$pearson_adj)

field_subgroup_by_mod <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  pr |>
    group_by(moderator) |>
    group_modify(~ signed_metrics(.x)) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  transmute(submission,
            value      = pearson_r,
            moderator  = factor(unname(moderator_label_map[moderator]),
                                levels = unname(moderator_label_map)),
            metric     = factor("Moderators"))
p_sub_field <- plot_metric_distribution(
  field_subgroup_by_mod, x = "moderator", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Moderators" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "B", y = "Pearson r")

(p_sub_scatter | p_sub_field) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.3))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-field-subgroup
#| tbl-cap: "Field summary of subgroup recovery (placeholder data, Tier 1): centre and spread across approaches of each signed-effect metric on the condition × moderator interaction estimates, pooled across the illustrative outcomes and six moderators (human replication reference excluded)."

field_subgroup <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  signed_metrics(pr) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels[c("directional_pct", "spearman_rho",
                                  "pearson_r", "pearson_adj")])

summarise_field(field_subgroup) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, median, sd, min, max), decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", median = "Median",
             sd = "SD", min = "Min", max = "Max")
```

##### Response-shape recovery

How well an approach reproduces the **shape** of the human response distribution, not only its mean. Using the preregistered `compare_distributions()` in the shared control condition, we score the overlap coefficient (OVL, 1 = identical densities), the **Wasserstein-1 distance** (W1, 0 = identical; the integral of the absolute ECDF difference, in scale points), the Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance (KS, 0 = indistinguishable), and the variance ratio (clone/human, 1 = matched spread) for each continuous outcome. W1 is the field's current standard distributional metric for bounded ordinal scales and is added here as the bandwidth-free companion to OVL: the KDE-based OVL depends on kernel bandwidths, which scale with the two samples' sizes (a large synthetic sample is smoothed differently from the human half) and leak density past the 0/100 scale bounds, whereas W1 has no tuning parameter and reads directly in scale points. The **variance ratio stays the headline diagnostic** (directional expectation: under-dispersion, ratio < 1). These are inherently per-outcome, control-condition quantities. @fig-distribution-headline therefore shows the raw response densities for every continuous outcome, one facet per outcome, with the field's median variance ratio stamped on each facet; all four metrics per outcome are in @tbl-field-distribution.

```{r}
#| label: distribution-pairs

dist_per_outcome <- function(pred_data) {
  map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
    compare_distributions(human_data_1, pred_data, out, control_lab,
                          include_w1 = TRUE) |>
      mutate(outcome = out)
  })
}

dist_labels <- c(ovl = "Overlap (OVL)", w1 = "Wasserstein-1",
                 ks_d = "KS distance", variance_ratio = "Variance ratio")

# Per-outcome shape metrics built once per submission, reused by the
# distribution figure and its companion table (cached: 29 x 11 KDE/ECDF
# passes over full respondent-level data).
dist_per_outcome_list <- cached("amendment_dist_per_outcome", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, dist_per_outcome)
})
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-distribution-headline
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-cap: "Response-shape recovery (placeholder data, Tier 1): control-condition response densities for every continuous outcome. In each facet, every Tier-1 approach is a thin line, Human 1 (the reference half) the bold line, Human 2 (the replication half) dashed red; the corner stamp is the field's median variance ratio for that outcome (VR; approach ÷ human, 1 = matched spread, below 1 = under-dispersion — the expected failure mode of synthetic samples). The full shape metrics per outcome are in the companion table."

vr_ann <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list,
                   ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y)) |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
  group_by(outcome) |>
  summarise(label = sprintf("VR = %.2f", median(variance_ratio, na.rm = TRUE)),
            .groups = "drop")

# wrapped primary-outcome label: the full one clips its facet strip
density_labels <- outcome_label_map
density_labels["trust_multidimensional"] <- "Multidimensional trust\n(primary)"

plot_density_overlay(
  human_data_1, human_data_2, llm_data_teams,
  outcomes       = outcomes_continuous,
  condition_val  = control_lab,
  outcome_labels = density_labels,
  annotations    = vr_ann,
  x_lab          = "Response (0–100, control condition)"
)
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-field-distribution
#| tbl-cap: "Field summary of response-shape recovery by outcome (placeholder data, Tier 1): median across approaches of each shape metric in the control condition, per continuous outcome; the human replication reference in parentheses. Ideal values: OVL = 1, W1 = 0, KS = 0, variance ratio = 1."

field_distribution <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list, function(d, sub) {
  d |>
    pivot_longer(c(ovl, w1, ks_d, variance_ratio),
                 names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  relabel_metrics(dist_labels)

field_group_table(field_distribution, "outcome", pct_metrics = character()) |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(outcome = "Outcome")
```

#### Finding 2 — Ranking approaches (secondary)

Having characterised the field, we rank individual approaches. This is the secondary, structural read: each metric is its own ranking, and the interesting result is *which* approaches lead on *which* rung. The leaderboard **table** is the main summary; the ranking *visual* for the key metric — with its cluster-bootstrap uncertainty intervals — is panel B of @fig-headline.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-leaderboard
#| tbl-cap: "Cross-team leaderboard (placeholder data). Pooled ATE-recovery metrics across all outcomes, plus calibration slope and R² on the primary outcome and interval scores for Tiers 2–3, scored against the Human 1 reference half. The Human replication row (Human 1 vs. Human 2) shows how well humans replicate humans at this sample size; it is a reference, not an upper bound — a precise submission can legitimately exceed it. Metrics run least-to-most strict, left to right; Class is the disclosure-class badge (A open / B escrowed / C sealed)."

leaderboard |>
  select(submission, tier, disclosure, directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r,
         pearson_adj, pearson_fz, inferential_pct, tost_pct, beta, calib_r2,
         pi_coverage_pct, interval_score) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj, pearson_fz,
                         beta, calib_r2, interval_score),
             decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(directional_pct, inferential_pct, tost_pct,
                         pi_coverage_pct), decimals = 0) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission      = "Submission",
    tier            = "Tier",
    disclosure      = "Class",
    directional_pct = "Directional %",
    spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
    pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
    pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
    pearson_fz      = "Pearson r (per-outcome)",
    inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
    tost_pct        = "TOST %",
    beta            = "Calib. β",
    calib_r2        = "Calib. R²",
    pi_coverage_pct = "PI coverage %",
    interval_score  = "Interval score"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations. Divergence from the pooled Pearson r flags a submission that predicts which outcomes move without ranking the interventions within them.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pearson_fz)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Tiers 2–3 only: coverage of the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals (honest intervals land near 95) and the mean Winkler interval score on the 0–100 outcomes (lower = better; a proper score, so neither padding intervals wide nor squeezing them tight pays).",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pi_coverage_pct)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "The floor's directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign — an artifact of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill.",
    locations = cells_body(columns = directional_pct,
                           rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f4f4f4"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  )
```

##### Cross-metric consistency

A single-metric verdict can mislead: an approach that leads on one rung may trail on another, and @cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b show empirically that performance on one data feature routinely fails to transfer to others. Rather than leave that to a reader's cross-panel comparison, we report it directly: the Spearman correlation between the approaches' rankings on each pair of ladder rungs (reference and floor rows excluded). High values mean the rungs sort the field similarly — a summary rank would be defensible; low or negative values mean each rung tells its own story and no single number should be trusted alone.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-metric-consistency
#| tbl-cap: "Cross-metric rank consistency (placeholder data): Spearman correlations between the approaches' rankings on each pair of pooled ladder metrics (reference and floor rows excluded). On placeholder data all scores are noise, so consistency is expected to be near zero."

metric_cols <- names(ladder_labels)

rank_consistency <- leaderboard |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication", !submission %in% floor_labels) |>
  select(all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  cor(method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

rank_consistency |>
  as_tibble(rownames = "metric") |>
  mutate(metric = unname(ladder_labels[metric])) |>
  rename_with(~ unname(ladder_labels[.x]), all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -metric, decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "")
```

#### Design analysis: what the metrics can resolve at this sample size

Before locking the metric ladder, we check — from design constants and literature priors only, touching no data — whether each rung has *dynamic range* at this design: whether a genuinely good submission can score visibly better than the floor. The inputs are the design's cell sizes (≈500 per intervention and ≈1,000 in control per human half), a typical 0–100 slider SD of ≈25 points, and plausible spreads of true megastudy ATEs (SDs of roughly 0.5–2 points; large message-based megastudies rarely move attitude scales by more). Three conclusions follow. **First**, the reference half-sample ATE SE is ≈1.4 points — the noise floor under every raw metric. **Second**, the expected human-replication correlation and the expected correlation of a *perfect* predictor can be computed for each plausible true-ATE spread; the perfect predictor's is always the square root of the replication row's, which is the quantitative form of "the replication reference is beatable" (see *Overview*), and when the true spread is small relative to 1.4 the r~adj~ NA-guard is expected to bind for many entries. **Third**, v1's relative TOST bound Δ = 0.5 × |ATE~h~| (≈0.25–1 point) is *smaller than the smallest difference the test could ever confirm* (1.645 × se~diff~ ≈ 2.3 points even for a noiseless submission), so that rung would read ~0% for every entry including the human replication row — a dead metric. The preregistered fixed bound of **3 scale points** (3% of range) gives a perfect predictor roughly 40% per-pair equivalence power while remaining unreachable for badly wrong predictions: dynamic range restored, at the cost of a laxer notion of equivalence, which we accept and state.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-design-analysis
#| tbl-cap: "Blinded design analysis (no data touched): expected values of key scoring quantities from design constants (cell sizes ≈500/1,000 per half, outcome SD ≈25) under three plausible spreads of true ATEs. r (replication) is the expected human-replication correlation; r (perfect) the expected raw correlation of an error-free predictor — its square root relationship to the replication value is why the reference row is beatable. TOST columns give the per-pair probability that a perfect predictor declares equivalence under the old relative bound and the new fixed bound Δ = 3."

sd_y     <- 25
n_arm_h  <- 500
n_ctrl_h <- 1000
se_ate_h <- sd_y * sqrt(1 / n_arm_h + 1 / n_ctrl_h)
z05      <- qnorm(0.95)

tost_power <- function(delta, se) {
  margin <- delta - z05 * se
  if (margin <= 0) 0 else pnorm(margin / se) - pnorm(-margin / se)
}

tibble(tau = c(0.5, 1, 2)) |>
  mutate(
    se_reference    = se_ate_h,
    r_replication   = tau^2 / (tau^2 + se_ate_h^2),
    r_perfect       = sqrt(r_replication),
    tost_relative   = map_dbl(0.5 * tau, tost_power, se = se_ate_h),
    tost_fixed_3    = tost_power(3, se_ate_h)
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -tau, decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(
    tau           = "SD of true ATEs (pts)",
    se_reference  = "Reference SE (pts)",
    r_replication = "r (replication)",
    r_perfect     = "r (perfect predictor)",
    tost_relative = "TOST power, relative Δ",
    tost_fixed_3  = "TOST power, fixed Δ = 3"
  )
```

#### Robustness of the scoring pipeline: split-seed sensitivity

The scoring pipeline has researcher degrees of freedom of its own — chiefly the preregistered random seed that splits the human sample into Human 1 and Human 2, and secondarily the TOST bound. @cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b show that such analytic choices can move fidelity conclusions substantially, and a skeptic could reasonably ask whether the reported reference value reflects the split we happened to draw. We therefore report a **resplit sensitivity check**: the half-split is redrawn many times with different seeds, the human replication reference is recomputed on each, and the spread of the reference's metrics across resplits is reported alongside the primary (preregistered-seed) values. A narrow spread demonstrates that the seed is not driving the results. When real data arrive, the same check is run for the full field: every submission is rescored against each resplit's Human 1, and the stability of the field summary (and of leaderboard ranks) across resplits is reported.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-resplit
#| tbl-cap: "Split-seed sensitivity of the human replication reference (placeholder data, 50 resplits): distribution of each pooled ATE-recovery metric for the Human 1 vs. Human 2 comparison across 50 alternative random half-splits, next to the value under the preregistered seed. On placeholder data the reference is itself noise-level, so the check demonstrates the code, not a substantive reference value. The 50 resplits are drawn from the same sample and so are mutually dependent; the spread quantifies split-conditional variability, not independent replications."

resplit_ceiling <- cached("amendment_resplit_ceiling", {
  all_ids <- unique(human_data$id)

  map_dfr(1:50, function(i) {
    set.seed(1000 + i)
    ids_i <- sample(all_ids, size = floor(length(all_ids) / 2))
    h1_i  <- human_data |> filter(id %in% ids_i)
    h2_i  <- human_data |> filter(!id %in% ids_i)

    side_of <- function(dat, suffix) {
      map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
        prep_ate(run_main_treatment_model(dat, outcome = out)) |>
          mutate(outcome = out)
      }) |>
        rename_with(~ paste0(.x, suffix), c(estimate, se, p_adj))
    }

    inner_join(side_of(h1_i, "_h"), side_of(h2_i, "_l"),
               by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
      mutate(infer_l = classify_sig(estimate_l, p_adj_l)) |>
      pooled_metrics() |>
      mutate(resplit = i)
  })
})

resplit_ceiling |>
  pivot_longer(-resplit, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(metric %in% names(ladder_labels)) |>
  group_by(metric) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            min = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),  max = max(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_defined = sum(!is.na(value)), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(
    # Restricted to the continuous outcomes for comparability with the
    # resplits (whose side_of() maps outcomes_continuous only).
    pairs_list[["Human replication"]] |>
      filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous) |>
      pooled_metrics() |>
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric",
                   values_to = "preregistered_seed"),
    by = "metric"
  ) |>
  mutate(metric = factor(unname(ladder_labels[metric]),
                         levels = unname(ladder_labels))) |>
  arrange(metric) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, sd, min, max, preregistered_seed), decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", sd = "SD", min = "Min",
             max = "Max", n_defined = "N defined",
             preregistered_seed = "Preregistered seed")
```

When real submissions arrive, both findings are recomputed by replacing the placeholder datasets with the submitted prediction files routed through their tier's builder (`build_pairs_t1()` / `build_pairs_t2()` / `build_pairs_t3()`); the subgroup and distribution figures are produced for the tiers eligible for them, alongside the demographic-baseline and stereotyping diagnostics of Section 3 — including the demographic parity gap (`demographic_parity_gap()`). The approach-characteristics table coded from the registration forms is then joined to the leaderboard for the exploratory analysis of which design choices accompany better predictions (publicly disclosed features only; Class C entries excluded from this analysis, not from the leaderboard).
